scope_and_ai_definitions.description: The precise definition of what constitutes an AI system and the overall scope of the regulation, including which actors are covered by its provisions.
scope_and_ai_definitions.salience: Industry seeks clarity and a narrow, technology-neutral definition to avoid over-regulation and maintain flexibility, while civil society and some Member States prefer a broader definition to ensure comprehensive coverage of potential risks.

high_risk_classification_criteria.description: The criteria and process for classifying AI systems as 'high-risk,' which triggers the most stringent requirements and obligations under the Act.
high_risk_classification_criteria.salience: AI developers and industry associations lobby for a precise and narrow scope of high-risk systems to limit compliance costs and administrative burdens, whereas civil society groups advocate for broader inclusion to enhance safety and fundamental rights protection.

prohibited_ai_practices_scope.description: The extent and specific types of AI systems and practices that are entirely prohibited due to their unacceptable risk to fundamental rights and democratic values.
prohibited_ai_practices_scope.salience: Civil society and human rights organizations push for extensive bans, particularly on surveillance technologies, while law enforcement agencies and some Member States seek exceptions or narrower prohibitions for security and public safety purposes.

conformity_assessment_and_enforcement.description: The mechanisms for assessing AI system conformity, the role of notified bodies versus self-assessment, and the framework for market surveillance and post-market monitoring.
conformity_assessment_and_enforcement.salience: Industry prefers more self-assessment to reduce burdens and speed market entry, while civil society and consumer protection groups advocate for robust independent third-party assessments and strong enforcement by national authorities.

innovation_and_sme_support.description: The measures designed to foster innovation, reduce regulatory burdens for SMEs, and promote AI development within the EU, such as regulatory sandboxes and specific support initiatives.
innovation_and_sme_support.salience: Start-ups, SMEs, and tech industry associations lobby for lighter regulatory touch, financial support, and streamlined processes to enhance competitiveness, while civil society insists that innovation should not compromise safety and ethical standards.

data_governance_and_quality.description: The requirements for the quality, representativeness, and governance of data used for training, validation, and testing high-risk AI systems to prevent bias and discrimination.
data_governance_and_quality.salience: AI developers seek flexibility in data acquisition and usage due to the sheer volume required, while civil society and data protection authorities demand strict rules to ensure data quality, prevent bias, and protect privacy.

human_oversight_and_fundamental_rights.description: The degree and nature of human oversight required for high-risk AI systems and the specific mechanisms to ensure respect for fundamental rights, including data protection and non-discrimination.
human_oversight_and_fundamental_rights.salience: Civil society organisations advocate for strong, meaningful human oversight and robust safeguards for fundamental rights, whereas industry players often highlight practical challenges and cost implications of extensive human intervention.

transparency_and_explainability.description: The obligations for AI system providers to ensure transparency, provide clear information to users, and make AI decisions explainable to affected individuals.
transparency_and_explainability.salience: Consumer groups and civil society demand high levels of transparency and explainability, crucial for trust and redress, while AI developers often argue this is technically challenging, can reveal proprietary information, and may be overly complex for end-users.

ai_in_public_services_and_law_enforcemen.description: The specific conditions and safeguards for the use of high-risk AI systems by public authorities, particularly in sensitive areas such as law enforcement, migration, and public administration.
ai_in_public_services_and_law_enforcemen.salience: Law enforcement agencies and some Member States seek flexibility for security and public safety applications, whereas civil liberties advocates and data protection bodies demand strict limitations, clear oversight, and strong fundamental rights protections.

scope_and_ai_definitions.description: The precise definition of what constitutes an AI system and the overall scope of the regulation, including which actors are covered by its provisions.

amendment_examples.body_excerpt: (44)	High data qualityisessential for the performance of many AI systems, especially when techniques involving the training of models are used, with a view to ensure that the high-risk AI system performs as intended and safely and it does not become the source of discrimination prohibited by Union l

(45)	For the development of high-risk AI systems, certain actors, such as providers, notified bodies and other relevant entities, such as digital innovation hubs, testing experimentation facilities and researchers, should be able to access and use high quality datasets within their respective fields

(30)	‘validation data’ means data used for providing an evaluation of the trained AI system and for tuning its non-learnable parameters and its learning process, among other things, in order to prevent overfitting; whereas the validation dataset can be a separate dataset or part of the training data

(31)	‘testing data’ means data used for providing an independent evaluation of the trained and validated AI system in order to confirm the expected performance of that system before its placing on the market or putting into service; (31)	‘testing data’ means data used for providing an independent ev
(44 h)'unfair bias' means an inclination of prejudice towards or against a natural person that can result in discriminatory and/or unfair treatment of some natural persons with respect to others.
Having regard to the joint opinion of the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor,

(41 a)AI systems do not operate in a lawless world.A number of legally binding rules at European, national and international level already apply or are relevant to AI systems today.Legal sources include, but are not limited to EU primary law (the Treaties of the European Union and its Charter of Fun

(42)	To mitigate the risks from high-risk AI systems placed or otherwise put into service on the Union market for users and affected persons, certain mandatory requirements should apply, taking into account the intended purpose of the use of the system and according to the risk management system to

scope_and_ai_definitions.summary: The Commission Proposal framed the regulation primarily around internal market functioning and a risk-based, proportionate approach to AI oversight. By the Text Adopted stage, Parliament's Amendment 3 reoriented the core purpose toward promoting 'human centric and trustworthy artificial intelligence' with explicit protection of health, safety, fundamental rights, democracy, and rule of law—shifting from a market-harmonisation framing to a rights-and-values framing.

stage_notes.note: Scope defined around market functioning: ensuring AI systems placed on the Union market are safe, respecting fundamental rights as a secondary concern. Emphasis on proportionality, minimal intervention, and a risk-based approach to avoid restricting trade or innovation.

Procedural document with joint committee procedure (IMCO + LIBE), signalling that civil liberties and fundamental rights would gain greater prominence in shaping scope and definitions alongside internal market considerations.

Amendment 3 to Recital 1 explicitly replaces 'improve the functioning of the internal market' with 'promote the uptake of human centric and trustworthy artificial intelligence' and elevates protection of fundamental rights, democracy, and rule of law. The addition of ECB and EDPB/EDPS opinions (Amendments 1–2) further broadens institutional oversight scope.

high_risk_classification_criteria.summary: The Commission Proposal established a relatively contained two-track classification system (Annex II product safety legislation and Annex III standalone high-risk use-cases), with a limited list of sectors. By the Parliament's adopted text in June 2023, the classification scope was substantially expanded: the EP introduced stricter self-assessment obligations, broadened the Annex III use-case list (notably in biometrics, law enforcement, and migration), added new criteria for classifying GPAI/foundation models, and shifted the overarching framing from market-functioning to fundamental rights protection, meaning more AI systems would meet the high-risk threshold.

stage_notes.note: Introduced a two-tier Annex structure: Annex II (AI as safety component in regulated products) and Annex III (eight standalone high-risk application areas). Classification was largely binary and self-administered by providers, with the Commission empowered to update Annex III via delegated acts. Criteria centred on severity of harm and affected population size.

Joint IMCO/LIBE committee (rapporteurs Benifei and Tudorache) proposed significant expansions: new prohibited practices were added, Annex III use-cases were broadened (e.g. emotion recognition, real-time biometric categorisation), requirements for foundation/general-purpose AI models were introduced as a new classification layer, and national market surveillance competences were strengthened. The committee also tightened the delegated-act conditions for updating the high-risk list.

Plenary amendments (June 2023) solidified and in some cases extended the committee position. Amendment 3 reframed the Regulation's core purpose around 'human centric and trustworthy AI' and explicit protection of 'health, safety, fundamental rights, democracy and rule of law,' raising the normative bar for what triggers high-risk classification. The adopted text retained expanded Annex III categories, kept GPAI obligations, and reinforced that classification decisions must account not just on intended use but also reasonably foreseeable misuse.

prohibited_ai_practices_scope.summary: The Commission Proposal established a risk-based framework with minimum necessary prohibitions focused on safety and fundamental rights compliance, leaving prohibited practices narrowly scoped. By the Text Adopted stage, the framing shifted toward 'trustworthy AI' as an overarching standard, and the legislative record shows expanded prohibited categories and stronger language around democratic values — though the excerpts provided focus on recitals rather than the operative prohibition articles directly.

stage_notes.note: Establishes a proportionate, risk-based approach explicitly limited to 'minimum necessary requirements.' The prohibition regime is framed defensively — avoiding over-regulation while addressing unjustified risks. The emphasis is on legal certainty and market access, suggesting a narrower initial scope for outright prohibitions.

The excerpt provided is the cover page only (A9-0188/2023, rapporteurs Benifei and Tudorache). The Parliament committee process is known to have substantially expanded prohibited practices — including bans on real-time remote biometric surveillance, emotion recognition in workplaces/schools, and social scoring — but the substantive amendment text is not visible in the supplied excerpt.

The recital language shifts from 'safe' AI to 'trustworthy' AI as the governing standard, and adds explicit reference to preventing harms to 'innovation, deployment and uptake' alongside rights protection. The broadened framing of trustworthiness as a baseline implies a wider normative foundation for prohibitions, consistent with an expanded final list of banned practices relative to the original proposal.

conformity_assessment_and_enforcement.summary: From the Commission Proposal to the Text Adopted, the framework for conformity assessment and enforcement shifted from a market-centric, proportionality-focused approach toward a rights-protective model with stronger oversight obligations. The EP's first-reading position reframed the regulation's purpose away from internal market harmonisation toward guaranteeing high-level protection of health, safety, fundamental rights, democracy and rule of law, signalling intent to tighten conformity and enforcement mechanisms beyond the Commission's minimalist baseline.

stage_notes.note: Established a risk-based, proportionate framework explicitly designed not to unduly constrain technological development or increase costs; enforcement was framed as supplementary to existing law, with the stated goal of legal certainty for market actors.

The joint IMCO/LIBE committee report (A9-0188/2023) introduced substantive amendments expanding the scope of high-risk categories and strengthening third-party conformity assessment requirements, including enhanced roles for notified bodies and post-market monitoring obligations.

The EP's adopted text (P9_TA(2023)0236) reoriented the regulation's core purpose toward fundamental rights protection and added stricter conformity assessment and market surveillance provisions, reflecting a deliberate strengthening of enforcement architecture relative to the Commission's original proposal.

innovation_and_sme_support.summary: The framing shifted from a purely market-harmonisation focus to explicitly embedding innovation support as a core objective alongside safety and rights protection. The Committee and final adopted text added explicit language on 'supporting innovation' and promoting 'uptake' of AI, signalling a stronger political commitment to enabling AI development rather than merely regulating it.

stage_notes.note: Innovation is mentioned as a societal benefit and competitive advantage in the explanatory memorandum, but the regulatory text focuses primarily on internal market harmonisation and fundamental rights protection; SME or innovation-specific measures are not yet prominent in the excerpted provisions.

The purpose clause is substantially rewritten to explicitly include 'supporting innovation' alongside safety and rights protection; a new recital (1a) is introduced to ensure benefits are distributed across society including companies, reflecting a stronger innovation-enabling orientation.

The final text retains the Committee's innovation-supportive language in the purpose clause, consolidating 'supporting innovation and improving the functioning of the internal market' as a twin objective, while also expanding cross-border provisions—indicating the innovation focus was maintained and formalised through the legislative process.

data_governance_and_quality.summary: The data governance and quality provisions were progressively strengthened from the Commission Proposal through the Committee Report to the Text Adopted, with the Parliament introducing more explicit human-centric framing and expanding oversight through references to data protection authorities. The final adopted text added formal consultation requirements for the European Data Protection Board and European Data Protection Supervisor, signaling heightened institutional attention to data quality and bias prevention in high-risk AI systems.

stage_notes.note: The proposal addressed bias and data quality indirectly through general references to fundamental rights, opacity, complexity, and unpredictability of AI systems, framing these as concerns to be addressed without yet specifying detailed data governance requirements.

The committee stage (A9-0188/2023) consolidated amendments from multiple committees including IMCO, LIBE, ITRE, CULT, and JURI, signaling a broadening of stakeholder input on data governance issues and setting the foundation for more detailed provisions on training data quality and representativeness for high-risk AI.

The adopted text explicitly added citations requiring opinions from the European Central Bank and a joint opinion from the European Data Protection Board and European Data Protection Supervisor, directly embedding data governance oversight into the legislative framework. Recital 1 was also reframed toward 'human centric and trustworthy' AI with high-level protection of fundamental rights, strengthening the normative basis for data quality and anti-bias requirements.

human_oversight_and_fundamental_rights.summary: The primary purpose of the regulation shifted from improving the internal market to explicitly promoting 'human centric and trustworthy' AI and ensuring high-level protection of fundamental rights, democracy, rule of law, and environment. The Committee Report and Text Adopted both added explicit reference to the joint opinion of the European Data Protection Board and European Data Protection Supervisor, and reframed the regulation's core objective around rights protection rather than market harmonisation.

stage_notes.note: Frames the regulation primarily as an internal market instrument. Retains all strengthening introduced at committee stage. The dual-column format confirms the final text adopts the committee's rights-centric framing over the original Commission language.
